blog_text:
For decades, Nevada’s Area 51 Air Force facility has represented the eye of a conspiratorial hurricane that swirls around “evidence” that aliens (and their technology) exist and are hiding behind its walls. Books, TV shows, and even massive online “raids” have tried to glimpse beyond its stark signs, warning against trespassers.

While aliens aren’t taking up residence in the compound, what is going on there is just as interesting.

In the middle of the barren Nevada desert, there’s a dusty unmarked road that leads to the front gate of Area 51. It’s protected by little more than a chain link fence, a boom gate, and intimidating trespassing signs. One would think that America’s much-mythicized, top-secret military base would be under closer guard, but make no mistake: they are watching.

Beyond the gate, cameras see every angle. On the distant hilltop, there’s a white pickup truck with a tinted windshield peering down on everything below. Locals says the base knows every desert tortoise and jackrabbit that hops the fence. Others claim there are embedded sensors in the approaching road.

What exactly goes on inside of Area 51 has led to decades of wild speculation. There are, of course, the alien conspiracies that galactic visitors are tucked away somewhere inside. One of the more colorful rumors insists the infamous 1947 Roswell crash was actually a Soviet aircraft piloted by mutated little people, and the wreckage remains on the grounds of Area 51. Some even believe the U.S. government filmed the 1969 moon landing in one of the base’s hangars.

For all the myths and legends, what’s true is that Area 51 is very real—and still very active. There may not be aliens or a moon-landing movie set beyond those fences, but something is going on, and only a select few are privy to what’s happening further down that closely monitored wind-swept Nevada road.

“The forbidden aspect of Area 51 is what makes people want to know what’s there,” says aerospace historian and author, Peter Merlin, who’s been researching Area 51 for more than three decades.

“And there sure is still a lot going on there.”

The Origins of a Mystery

The beginning of Area 51 is directly related to the development of the U-2 reconnaissance aircraft. After World War II, the Soviet Union lowered the Iron Curtain around itself and the rest of the Eastern bloc, creating a near intelligence blackout to the rest of the world. When the Soviets backed North Korea’s invasion of South Korea in June 1950, it became increasingly clear that the Kremlin would aggressively expand its influence. America worried about the USSR’s technology, intentions, and ability to launch a surprise attack—only a decade removed from the Japanese attack on Pearl Harbor.

In the early 1950s, the U.S. Navy and U.S. Air Force sent low-flying aircraft on reconnaissance missions over the USSR, but they were at constant risk of being shot down. In November 1954, President Eisenhower approved the secret development of a high-altitude reconnaissance aircraft called the U-2 program. One of the first orders of business was to track down a remote, covert location for training and testing. They found it in the southern Nevada desert near a salt flat known as Groom Lake, which had once been a World War II aerial gunnery range for Army Air Corps pilots.

Known by its map designation as Area 51, this middle-of-nowhere site became a new top-secret military base. To convince workers to come, Kelly Johnson, one of the leading engineers of the U-2 project, gave it a more enticing name: Paradise Ranch.

Making a Myth

U-2 testing began in July 1955, and immediately, reports came flooding in about unidentified flying object sightings. If you read the details in a 1992 CIA report that was declassified with redactions in 1998 (and subsequently released nearly in full in 2013), it’s easy to see why.

Many of these sightings were observed by commercial airline pilots who had never seen an aircraft fly at such high altitudes as the U-2. Whereas today’s airliners can soar as high as 45,000 feet, in the mid-1950s, airlines flew at altitudes between 10,000 and 20,000 feet. Known military aircraft could get to 40,000 feet, and some believed manned flight couldn’t go any higher than that. The U-2, flying at altitudes in excess of 60,000 feet, would’ve looked completely alien.

Naturally, Air Force officials knew the majority of these unexplained sightings were U-2 tests, but they were not allowed to reveal these details to the public. So, “natural phenomena” or “high-altitude weather research” became go-to explanations for UFO sightings, including in 1960 when Gary Powers’ U-2 was shot down over Russia.

What’s also interesting about the most recent 2013 report is that it confirms Area 51’s existence. While the 1998 version does have significant redactions when referencing the name and location of the U-2 test site, the nearly un-redacted version from 2013 reveals much more, including multiple references to Area 51, Groom Lake, and even a map of the area.

“This Is Earth Technology”

U-2 operations halted in the late 1950s, but other top-secret military aircraft continued tests at Area 51. Over the years, the A-12 and numerous stealth aircraft like Bird of Prey, F-117A, and TACIT BLUE have all been developed and tested in the Nevada desert. More declassified documents reveal Area 51’s role in “Project Have Doughnut,” a 1970s attempt to study covertly obtained Soviet MiGs.

“They flew them [over Area 51] .. .and pitted our own fighters against them to develop tactics,” says Merlin. “They learned that you can’t out-turn it, but you can outrun it. And it’s still going on today. ... Now, instead of seeing MiG-17s and 21s, there’s MiG-29s and SU-27s.”

The flights are ongoing. In September 2017, an Air Force Lt. Col. was killed under mysterious circumstances when his plane crashed in Nevada and the Pentagon would not immediately ID the aircraft. It seems he was most likely flying a foreign jet obtained by the United States.

Even so, the alien conspiracies gained ground in 1989 when Bob Lazar claimed in an interview on Las Vegas local news that he’d seen aliens, and had helped to reverse-engineer alien spacecraft while working at the base. Many have disregarded this as fiction, and are even offended at the notion—including Merlin, who has spent years talking with former Area 51 engineers and employees angered by all the fuss about E.T.

“Some are even mad because they worked on these things and built these amazing planes,” Merlin says. “This is Earth technology. You got folks claiming it’s extraterrestrial when it’s really good old American know-how.”

The Truth Is Out There

Today, Area 51 is still very much in use. According to Google Earth, new construction and expansions are continuously happening. On most early mornings, eagle-eyed visitors can spot strange lights in the sky moving up and down. No, it’s not a UFO; it’s actually the semi-secret contract commuter airline using the call sign “Janet” that transports workers from Las Vegas’s McCarran Airport to the base.

As for what’s happening these days in America’s most secretive military base, few know for sure. Merlin has some educated guesses, including improved stealth technology, advanced weapons, electronic warfare systems and, in particular, unmanned aerial vehicles. Chris Pocock, noted U-2 historian and author of several books about the matter, told Popular Mechanics he thinks classified aircraft, more exotic forms of radio communication, directed energy weapons, and lasers are currently under development at the base.

While the lore around Area 51 may be nothing more than imaginative fiction, that won’t stop people from gawking just beyond those chain link fences. “At the most basic level, anytime you have something secret or forbidden, it’s human nature,” says Merlin. “You want to find out what it is.”

🛸 How to Explore Area 51

Fact or fiction, aliens are a big tourism draw. In 1996, the state of Nevada renamed Route 375 as the “Extraterrestrial Highway,” and destinations such as the Alien Research Center and the Little A’Le’Inn (in the town of Rachel with a population of around 54) dot the road.

To Area 51’s west, there’s the Alien Cathouse, which is advertised as the only alien-themed brothel in the world. Geocaching also attracts visitors here, since the highway is considered a “mega-trial” with over 2,000 geocaches hidden in the area.

Then there’s the actual base. While getting inside is not in the cards for most, curious civilians can actually drive up to front and back gates. Locals will direct you, and the website Dreamland Resort is a great resource full of maps, driving directions, and first-hand accounts.

However, one should be careful when planning a trek to Area 51. It’s the desert, after all, so bring plenty of water, snacks, and have proper weather gear—for the hot days and the cold nights. Phone service and GPS probably won’t work, so have printouts and actual maps. Gas stations are few and far in between, so carry spare fuel and tires.

Also, remember the government doesn’t really want you peering into Area 51. Both Merlin and Pocock confirmed that they have been closely observed or even intimidated by guards and security (including an F-16 fly-by). Do not trespass under any circumstances, or arrests and heavy fines await you.

Editor’s Note: This story was originally published on September 14, 2017.

Matt is a history, science, and travel writer who is always searching for the mysterious and hidden. He's written for Smithsonian Magazine, Washingtonian, Atlas Obscura, and Arlington Magazine. He calls Washington D.C. home and probably tells way too many cat jokes.

metadata.summary: The blog explores the history and persistent mythos surrounding Area 51, the top-secret military base in Nevada. While it debunks the popular notion of alien presence, it highlights the intriguing activities that have contributed to its mysterious reputation.

currency:
## Currency Analysis

**1. Publication/Update Date**
The publication or update date is not provided, so it's unclear if the information is current. This lack of visibility makes it difficult to assess the timeliness of the topic.

**2. Currency for the Field**
The topic of Area 51 and its associated conspiracy theories generally does not change rapidly, as it pertains to longstanding myths. Without a publication date, it's uncertain if the content reflects the most recent developments or discussions.

**3. Links and References**
The referenced links appear functional and lead to relevant articles or information. There are no visible signs of broken or outdated references based on the available text.

**4. Maintenance**
There is no indication of active maintenance, update notices, or revision history present in the text. The presence of recent comments cannot be assessed.

**5. Obsolescence Risk**
The content does not rely on outdated technology or terminology; it discusses longstanding theories and stories surrounding Area 51, which don't rapidly become obsolete.
accuracy_text:
## Accuracy Analysis

**1. Sources and Evidence**
The text provides various references, such as links to articles on Popular Mechanics about “raids” on Area 51, conspiracy theories, and military bases. It also mentions a Wikipedia link about boom barriers and references to history and CNN articles about the Roswell crash and moon landing hoax.

**2. Errors, Contradictions, and Inconsistencies**
There are no obvious factual errors or contradictions in the text. The piece acknowledges existing conspiracy theories and rumors about Area 51 but clarifies that they are speculative.

**3. Facts vs. Opinions**
The text blends facts and opinions without explicitly distinguishing them. For example, it uses terms like "what's true is that Area 51 is very real—and still very active," which presents factual information without hedging, while the discussion of conspiracies is clearly speculative.

**4. Verifiability**
The main factual claim about the existence and activity status of Area 51 can be verified through reputable sources like governmental releases and credible journalistic investigations. Conspiracy-related content, while verifiable in terms of its existence as a belief, does not stand up to evidence-based scrutiny.

facts.fact: Massive online 'raids' have tried to glimpse beyond Area 51, despite warnings against trespassers.
facts.verification:
## Fact Check

**Claim:** Massive online 'raids' have tried to glimpse beyond Area 51, despite warnings against trespassers.

**Verdict:** TRUE

**Justification:**  
Numerous reputable sources describe how a viral Facebook event labeled "Storm Area 51, They Can't Stop All of Us" prompted a surge of interest, with millions responding online and many travelers arriving near the military facility despite clear warnings from the U.S. Air Force and law enforcement against trespassing ([time.com](https://time.com/5627283/area-51-invasion-military-free-aliens/?utm_source=openai)). Though largely intended as satire, the event did result in some participants attempting to enter, and even a few arrests occurred ([time.com](https://time.com/5682078/area-51-raid/?utm_source=openai)). The phenomenon matches the description of massive online “raids” prompted by internet culture’s fascination with Area 51, undertaken despite stern warnings ([aol.com](https://www.aol.com/news/real-story-behind-myth-area-174605838.html?utm_source=openai)).

**References:**
- TIME, “The Military Has a Warning for Anyone Interested In Raiding Area 51…”  
- Wikipedia, “Storm Area 51”  
- AOL News, “As Area 51 raid becomes a joke, the U.S. Air Force issues a real warning”  
- TIME, “The Area 51 Raid Is (Sort of) on as People Share Staged Videos”  
- Snopes, “Are Photos of the ‘Area 51 Raid’ Real?”  
- IBTimes, “Area 51 Raids: How Many People Actually Showed Up To Raid The Secretive Facility?”  
- AOL, “The Real Story Behind the Myth of Area 51, America’s Most Famous Top‑Secret Military Base”

facts.fact: The front gate of Area 51 is protected by a chain link fence, a boom gate, and trespassing signs.
facts.verification:
## Fact Check

**Claim:** The front gate of Area 51 is protected by a chain link fence, a boom gate, and trespassing signs.

**Verdict:** PARTIALLY TRUE

**Justification:** A 2017 narrative describing Area 51’s front entrance states it is "protected by little more than a chain link fence, a boom gate, and intimidating trespassing signs" ([thediagonal.com](https://thediagonal.com/2017/02/08/area-51-lives-on/?utm_source=openai)), which supports the claim. However, more detailed sources indicate that while the front (“Groom Lake Road”) gate features warning signs (including “Use of deadly force authorized”), the perimeter is often marked by orange posts and warning signage rather than a continuous chain link fence, and physical barriers may not be consistently present ([science.howstuffworks.com](https://science.howstuffworks.com/space/aliens-ufos/area-512.htm?utm_source=openai)). Thus, the description captures part of the reality but oversimplifies the variable and more complex security setup.

**References:**
- Area 51 Lives On – "…protected by little more than a chain link fence, a boom gate, and intimidating trespassing signs."  
- How Area 51 Works (HowStuffWorks) – perimeter marked by orange posts and warning signs, not necessarily fenced.  
- Area‑51 Viewers Guide – restricted area marked by signs and posts, often without fences.

facts.fact: Locals claim the base knows every desert tortoise and jackrabbit that hops the fence, and that there are embedded sensors in the road.
facts.verification:
## Fact Check

**Claim:** Locals claim the base knows every desert tortoise and jackrabbit that hops the fence, and that there are embedded sensors in the road.

**Verdict:** PARTIALLY TRUE

**Justification:**  
There is credible evidence that military bases (e.g., U.S. Air Force) use advanced surveillance and sensor systems, such as the Tactical Automated Security System (TASS), which includes seismic, magnetic, infrared, and radar sensors—potentially enabling very detailed monitoring of movement around perimeters, including animals near fences ([en.wikipedia.org](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Tactical_Automated_Security_System?utm_source=openai)). Additionally, systems like the Multispectral Desert Fauna Surveillance and Recognition System have been developed to detect and identify wildlife movements in desert areas using infrared and visible cameras ([cybernet.com](https://www.cybernet.com/programs/multispectral-desert-fauna-surveillance-and-recognition-system/?utm_source=openai)). However, there is no direct evidence that bases literally know *every* individual desert tortoise or jackrabbit that passes the fence, nor that sensors are specifically embedded *in the road* to track these animals. The monitoring technologies referenced are focused on perimeter detection and wildlife census, not exhaustive identification of every individual animal or embedded road sensors.

**References:**
- Tactical Automated Security System (TASS) – U.S. Air Force sensor suite including seismic, infrared, radar, and more ([en.wikipedia.org](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Tactical_Automated_Security_System?utm_source=openai))  
- Multispectral Desert Fauna Surveillance and Recognition System – Wildlife detection using visible and infrared cameras plus image processing ([cybernet.com](https://www.cybernet.com/programs/multispectral-desert-fauna-surveillance-and-recognition-system/?utm_source=openai))

purpose.tone: The tone is primarily opinion-driven and slightly sensational. Phrases like “eye of a conspiratorial hurricane” and “make no mistake: they are watching” suggest a dramatic narrative.
purpose.style: The writing style is conversational and slightly journalistic. The use of vivid imagery and anecdotal evidence, such as "Locals say the base knows every desert tortoise and jackrabbit that hops the fence," supports this.
purpose.bias: There is a mild bias towards endorsing conspiracies by mentioning them in a somewhat intriguing manner without critical analysis, such as referring to "the eye of a conspiratorial hurricane."
purpose.sentiment: The sentiment is mixed. It combines elements of intrigue and skepticism, engaging with conspiracy theories without explicitly supporting or debunking them.
justifications: Phrases such as "eye of a conspiratorial hurricane" and "make no mistake: they are watching" indicate an opinion-driven and dramatic tone.
The conversational style is seen in phrases like "Locals say the base knows every desert tortoise and jackrabbit."
The mention of conspiracy theories in an intriguing way indicates mild bias, as seen with "the eye of a conspiratorial hurricane."
The overall approach suggests curiosity and intrigue, which corresponds to a mixed sentiment.

author_authority.positive_mentions: Matt Blitz is a highly respected and award‑winning production professional, currently serving as Executive Vice President and Executive Director of Agency Production at FCB Chicago (appointed May 9, 2024) ([screenmag.com](https://screenmag.com/fcb-chicago-appoints-matt-blitz-executive-director-of-agency-production/?utm_source=openai)). He previously held leadership roles at DDB Chicago (Head of Production; over four years as SVP, Executive Producer) and at Leo Burnett with 15 years of progressive production experience ([screenmag.com](https://screenmag.com/fcb-chicago-appoints-matt-blitz-executive-director-of-agency-production/?utm_source=openai)). Throughout his career, his teams have earned over 400 creative awards including Cannes Lions, D&AD, The One Show, Clio, and London International Awards ([screenmag.com](https://screenmag.com/fcb-chicago-appoints-matt-blitz-executive-director-of-agency-production/?utm_source=openai)). He is also recognized for specific high‑profile campaigns such as Samsung’s “Ostrich” and Skittles' “Broadway the Rainbow” ([screenmag.com](https://screenmag.com/fcb-chicago-appoints-matt-blitz-executive-director-of-agency-production/?utm_source=openai)).
author_authority.negative_mentions: No controversies, legal disputes, regulatory actions, or significant negative press were found in publicly available sources relating to Matt Blitz in the context of his professional role at Hearst Communications or elsewhere.
author_authority.sources: • Screen Magazine (FCB Chicago appointment) ([screenmag.com](https://screenmag.com/fcb-chicago-appoints-matt-blitz-executive-director-of-agency-production/?utm_source=openai)) • LBBOnline (interviews and production leadership at DDB Chicago, awards) ([lbbonline.com](https://lbbonline.com/news/ddbs-matt-blitz-on-building-the-production-plane-while-flying-it?utm_source=openai)) • LBB profile (general roles and news credits) ([lbbonline.com](https://lbbonline.com/people/matt-blitz?utm_source=openai))
author_authority.summary: Matt Blitz is a distinguished production executive with a robust career in creative advertising and agency leadership. He has held major roles at Leo Burnett, DDB Chicago, and most recently at FCB Chicago as Executive Director of Agency Production. His work has earned extensive recognition, with over 400 industry awards, and includes standout campaigns like Samsung’s “Ostrich” and Skittles’ “Broadway the Rainbow.” Recent coverage of his appointment at FCB Chicago (May 9, 2024) underscores his continued relevance in high-level agency production leadership ([screenmag.com](https://screenmag.com/fcb-chicago-appoints-matt-blitz-executive-director-of-agency-production/?utm_source=openai)).
author_authority.justification: Based on authoritative industry sources (Screen Magazine, LBBOnline, LBB), Matt Blitz has a strong professional reputation characterized by leadership roles, award‑winning production work, and recognition for technical innovation and creative execution. No credible sources indicate any controversies or concerns, suggesting a clean public record. While his affiliation with Hearst Communications was specified by the user, our research did not confirm any current or past connection to Hearst; all discovered roles are within advertising agencies.

publisher_authority.ownership_and_funding:
Hearst Communications, Inc. is a privately held multinational media and business information conglomerate founded in 1887 by William Randolph Hearst. It remains under the ownership of the Hearst family, administered through a trust structure with a board of thirteen trustees—five family members and eight external trustees—as dictated by William Randolph Hearst’s will. This trust structure ensures stewardship continuity, with the trust to dissolve only after all original heirs have passed. ([en.wikipedia.org](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hearst_Communications?utm_source=openai))

In recent years, Hearst has diversified its revenue away from purely consumer media toward business-to-business (B2B) services such as financial data (Fitch Group), healthcare solutions (QGenda, Hearst Health), and transportation data, which now constitute a majority (~60%) of profits as of 2025. ([axios.com](https://www.axios.com/2024/11/26/hearst-media-profit-b2b?utm_source=openai))

Financial transparency is typical of private companies: while revenue figures (e.g., $13 billion in 2024) and business breakdowns are publicly reported, detailed investor-level disclosures are limited. Hearst’s political contributions and lobbying expenditures—$520,000 in 2024—are reported publicly, though direct influence or alignment remains moderately opaque. ([freepress.net](https://www.freepress.net/who-owns-media/newspaper-publishing/who-owns-media-hearst?utm_source=openai))
publisher_authority.editorial_standards:
Hearst is a diversified media organization encompassing newspapers (e.g., San Francisco Chronicle, Houston Chronicle), magazines (Cosmopolitan, Esquire, Car and Driver), television networks (via A+E Global Media joint venture and ESPN stake), broadcasting stations, and digital ventures. ([en.wikipedia.org](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hearst_Communications?utm_source=openai))

While Hearst publications typically host editorial guidelines and corrections policies on individual titles, there is no consolidated, company-wide editorial standards page centrally accessible. This reflects a decentralized editorial model that varies across divisions and brands.

There is no evidence of a formalized group-level universal corrections or anonymous-sources policy available publicly. However, the breadth of Hearst’s participation in established journalistic networks (e.g., memberships or awards in major outlets under its umbrella) suggests varying adherence to standard journalism practices across its entities,

In terms of internal practice concerns: In 2023, Hearst instituted a social-media policy at some newsroom divisions that restricted journalists from expressing personal political opinions online—even off-duty—a move criticized by the Writers Guild-East as overreaching and potentially suppressing individual expression. ([freepress.net](https://www.freepress.net/who-owns-media/newspaper-publishing/who-owns-media-hearst?utm_source=openai))
publisher_authority.political_bias:
Major external media bias aggregators such as Media Bias/Fact Check, AllSides, and Ad Fontes Media do not evaluate conglomerate-level entities like Hearst Communications; rather, they rate individual media properties (e.g., San Francisco Chronicle or Esquire). As such, Hearst as a holding company lacks a specific bias rating.

However, individual Hearst-owned outlets vary broadly across the political spectrum—from centrist to left-leaning (e.g., lifestyle and local newspapers)—making generalization inaccurate. No systemic ideological bias at the corporate trust level has been documented.
publisher_authority.factual_reliability:
Similarly, no comprehensive, enterprise-level factual reliability assessment exists for Hearst Communications as a corporate entity. Reliability evaluations are conducted per outlet basis—for instance, local newspapers have varying track records of corrections and sourcing.

Notably, Hearst did not raise flags in major structure-level credibility reviews; definitional reliability assessments (like NewsGuard’s) focus on individual titles rather than the corporation. ([en.wikipedia.org](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/NewsGuard?utm_source=openai))
publisher_authority.public_perception:
Hearst has a longstanding reputation as a major mainstream media organization, with historical roots in yellow journalism, evolving over decades into a diversified conglomerate. Its consumer media outlets have been recognized with industry awards (e.g., Pulitzers for some titles), though specific recent awards require outlet-level research.

Among journalists, Hearst is viewed as a legacy media brand with strong business media presence; no singular criticism toward corporate credibility is widespread. However, the social-media policy controversy in 2023 sparked criticism about internal editorial freedom. ([freepress.net](https://www.freepress.net/who-owns-media/newspaper-publishing/who-owns-media-hearst?utm_source=openai))
publisher_authority.summary:
Hearst Communications, Inc. operates as a highly diversified, trustworthy media and information conglomerate with strong financial footing and respected brand legacy. Its ownership structure—via family-trust governance—ensures continuity and relative independence, though detailed financial transparency is limited compared to public companies. Hearst’s editorial infrastructure is decentralized, with varying practices across its many media subsidiaries; readers should evaluate each outlet individually for editorial rigor and fact-check policies.

Because Hearst is not directly rated by external bias or reliability agencies, assessing its credibility depends on outlet-level examination. Overall, as a corporate entity, Hearst merits a credibility rating of Medium–High, reflective of its long-standing market presence, reputable individual brands, and diversified focus on business information services. Key considerations for readers include verifying trustworthiness at the outlet level, being aware of individual editorial policies and corrections transparency, and noting internal controversies such as the social-media policy which may affect newsroom morale and openness.

raw_metadata.title: The Real Story Behind the Myth of Area 51, America’s Most Famous Top-Secret Military Base
raw_metadata.author: Matt Blitz
raw_metadata.description: There might not be aliens inside, but the truth is just as interesting.
tags: area 51, area 51 nevada, where is area 51, area 51 aliens, conspiracy theories, area 51 conspiracy
